Did you know that some of our most common queries can be solved 24/7 through our new self-service portal My Tower? With My Tower, customers with Tower house, contents, and car policies can manage their insurance online. No more waiting to speak to a person! * Make a payment or check when your next payment is due * View your policy details, upcoming renewals and all your policy documents * Change your address, sum insured, excess, or interested party * Update or change your bank account and credit card * Make a claim – it’s easier than you might think! body_preview: Hi Raffaela, From my Assessment I am satisfied that there is no loss of structural integrity to the downstairs of your property. David from Asset Restore feels the same which is why he and his team are happy to get in to clean the property. Once all remaining debris is removed from site we will look to get 2-3 quotes from separate Tower preferred builders. While these builders are on site, they will do a thorough inspection and if for some reason they feel that a structural engineer is required, then at that stage we would engage one. I have since authorised for Asset Restore to commence works plus have asked for an electrician and gas contractor to attend. I’ve also emailed David Tallot your questions and he has sent the below response. To answer the questions bellow, * I will engage an electrician to do a make safe before we start our work – we can do our work from generators if needed. * It’s up to you on the engineer, I didn’t see anything that would make me think there is a risk of collapse, while working onsite when linings come down if anything is noted I can get a builder to put props in, and we can then let an engineer inspect the site. * I Have allowed to remove the heavily smoke damaged asbestos as per photo which is less than 10m2. – the only risk is that I can see is if you need to remove the entire quantity to match a product that would be installed next to it this would increase quantity being removed to greater than 10m2 requiring a clearance. – considering it

body_preview: Hi Chris, Thank you again for sending through the Asset Restore scope of works and for all your efforts in helping move my claim forward. I’ve reviewed the document and truly appreciate the work being done to progress the reinstatement for 23 Mays Street, Devonport. As we discussed over the phone, before we proceed, I’d like to raise a few questions—I hope you don’t mind me checking in this way. My goal is to ensure everything is addressed safely and in the right order: A qualified master electrician has assessed the house and advised it’s not electrically safe, especially with the new switchboard and wiring. Has a formal licensed electrical inspection been scheduled to confirm safety before re-entry? As we spoke, there are structural beam damages, and the significant smoke traveling through floors, walls, and out all sides of the roof suggests extensive heat stress. The wetness and dampness from firefighters might have worsened these structural issues. Would it be prudent to have a structural engineer assess the building before any removal or cleaning begins? Regarding asbestos, the quote mentions less than 10m² of ACM material for removal, but the attached photo shows the damaged lower deck section is closely connected to the main deck, possibly indicating more extensive involvement. Would a full asbestos inspection be a wise step to rule out broader risks? The gas unit, exposed to heat and smoke, concerns me. Has a technician been arranged to inspect the system and fitting

body_preview: Hi Sasha, I hope you’re doing well! Thank you so much for all the support you’ve provided with my claim for 23 Maysi St, Devonport—it really means a lot. Following our chat on June 18, I wanted to kindly share some expenses I’ve incurred due to the fire, and I’d love your advice on the next steps. Here’s what I’ve spent so far, with proofs/invoices attached: $1,452 at Esplanade Hotel for 6 nights of temporary accommodation $5,000 for rent (at $1,000 per week) $727.38 for moving to the rental property $340 for cleaning some clothes $454.25 for sofa cleaning Would it be possible to get these reimbursed under my contents and/or house insurance? I’d also be grateful if you could let me know if the distress fund (up to $3,000 as per the policy) might be available, given how challenging this has been. On another note, I’ve just sent Chris an email about some safety concerns and assessment ideas we touched on earlier. I’d really appreciate it if we could align this with the plan for both contents and house insurance. Could you share what’s next and perhaps a rough timeline? Thanks again for your help. I forward to hearing from you! Best regards, Raffaella ￼￼￼￼￼

body_preview: Good afternoon David and Chris, Thank you for your follow-up email, David, and for clarifying the misunderstanding from our call. I appreciate your flexibility, and since you’re busy on Tuesday, Wednesday at 2:00 PM works for me—please confirm if that suits. Before we proceed, I want to reiterate two key priorities to ensure a safe and effective process: Assessment-Focused Work: Initial tasks should enable damage assessment (e.g., securing the site, removing debris for inspection) rather than repairs (e.g., cleaning curtains or upholstery), allowing me to exercise my right to an independent assessment and for both parties to agree on a remediation plan. Health & Safety (H&S) Risk Mitigation: No work should start until H&S risks are identified and addressed by independent experts, given the master electrician’s finding that the property is uninhabitable due to electrical hazards. I’ve been told by firefighters that there was substantial fire damage and significant smoke exiting the roof—initially thought to originate there by Devonport Flagstaff—which penetrated the house structure. Yet, the roof, the gas unit beneath it, and overall structural integrity remain uninspected. This, alongside asbestos and moisture concerns, raises serious safety issues. I’m particularly worried about the asbestos plan. The scope mentions removing less than 10m², but the deck’s fire-damaged section connects to the main area, suggesting potential wider involvement. body_preview: Good afternoon David and Chris, Thank you for your follow-up email, David, and for clarifying the misunderstanding from our call. I appreciate your flexibility, and since you’re busy on Tuesday, Wednesday at 2:00 PM works for me—please confirm if that suits. Before we proceed, I want to reiterate two key priorities to ensure a safe and effective process: Health & Safety (H&S) Risk Mitigation: No work should start until H&S risks are identified and addressed by independent experts, given the master electrician’s finding that the property is uninhabitable due to electrical hazards. Assessment-Focused Work: Initial tasks should enable damage assessment (e.g., securing the site, removing debris for inspection) rather than repairs (e.g., cleaning curtains or upholstery), allowing me to exercise my right to an independent assessment and for both parties to agree on a remediation plan. I’ve been told by firefighters that there was substantial fire damage and significant smoke exiting the roof—initially thought to originate there by Devonport Flagstaff—which penetrated the house structure. Yet, the roof, the gas unit beneath it, and overall structural integrity remain uninspected. This, alongside asbestos and moisture concerns, raises serious safety issues. I’m particularly worried about the asbestos plan. The scope mentions removing less than 10m², but the deck’s fire-damaged section connects to the main area, suggesting potential wider involvement. I raised this with Chris, request

body_preview: Hi Davi and Chris, I’m concerned by the suggestion of meeting at 1:00 p.m. on Wednesday or Thursday, when we had previously agreed on 2:00 p.m. Wednesday. That said, I also want to express that I believe a meeting may be premature. Several important points I’ve raised remain unaddressed, and without a clear written agreement on scope and priorities, I’m not sure an in-person meeting will be the best use of time. To ensure we move forward in a transparent, safe, and well-coordinated way, I would prefer that all communication from this point be by email only, with responses expected within 48 hours, unless otherwise agreed. Before any work proceeds, I would appreciate written confirmation of the following: A proper assessment of the current condition of the property must come first. This includes external review of any safety-related issues such as electrical risks, structural concerns, the gas unit, possible asbestos (noting the area by the deck), and any mould or water damage. These are essential to manage health and safety risks and ensure work is done properly. Initial work should focus on removing debris and internal linings to enable a complete assessment. Tasks like general cleaning (e.g. soft furnishings, heat pump cleaning, curtains, etc) that aren't related to preparing for assessment should not proceed yet. body_preview: Good morning Grant, Thank you so much for your email and for sharing the process document for my claim (C90306048) at 23 Mays Street, Devonport. I really appreciate your effort to outline the framework and the chance to work together on this. It’s great to see the team involved, and I’d love your guidance on how we can move forward smoothly. I’ve looked over the process map and have a few thoughts to ensure we get this right, especially around safety and planning. I’ve heard from firefighters about significant fire damage and smoke exiting the roof—initially thought to originate there by Devonport Flagstaff—which has me concerned about the house’s condition. Could we please consider the following adjustments? Clarify Claim Scope: Is this the plan for both house and contents claims? If so, should it therefore be linked to both policy numbers to ensure all coverage is included? Prioritize Health & Safety: I’d feel more comfortable if we scheduled independent assessments (electrical, structural, gas, moisture/mould) before any major work, alongside the July 9 asbestos survey. The master electrician’s finding that the property is uninhabitable due to electrical hazards is a key concern. Assessment-Only Focus: As previously expressed, could we limit initial tasks to enabling damage assessment (e.g., securing the site, debris removal) rather than repairs (e.g., upstairs decontamination, curtain cleaning)? This would allow for a thorough evaluation and mutual agreement on remediatio

We are now ready to move onto the next stage in the process which will be to undertake and complete as follows: Contents Appraisal Contents Removal Full decontamination clean upstairs level. Below I have provided answers in blue to your recent questions. Which I trust will allow us to move forward with the claim progression. * Clarify Claim Scope: Is this the plan for both house and contents claims? If so, should it therefore be linked to both policy numbers to ensure all coverage is included? The structural claim will require a scope -of works which is identified in the process list. The contents claim will have a schedule of contents determined. This is also listed I the process list provided. The 2 do not need to be linked. They can be cross referenced if needed. * Prioritize Health & Safety: I'd feel more comfortable if we scheduled independent assessments (electrical, structural, gas, moisture/mould) before any major work, alongside the July 9 asbestos survey. body_preview: Tower Insurance Let's get this claim sorted Hi Maria Thanks for letting us know about your claim. We're keen to get you back on the road as quickly as possible. Next steps – assessing the damage to your vehicle Your chosen repairer, Shields Bros Panel & Paint Ltd - Takapuna will contact you soon about your vehicle assessment. You can contact them directly at any time on 094896745; just remember to have your claim number C90316958 handy. You'll likely need to bring your vehicle in to the repairer, but sometimes they can assess the damage remotely. Once the assessment is done, the repairer will give us an update. We then work out how to best proceed with your claim and will be in touch with you to discuss the next steps. Do you need to pay an excess? The excess is the amount of any claim you've agreed to pay and you have a $1000 excess. Other excesses may also apply. We'll work through your claim and let you know if you need to pay an excess, and if so, what the amount would be. You can then pay the excess to the repairer when you pick up your vehicle or directly to Tower. If proven that another party was more than 50% at fault, and you're able to provide that party's contact details (name, phone number and registration number), you won’t have to pay an excess. Questions? You'll be assigned one of our dedicated Claims Managers. If you have any questions about your claim, you can login to My Tower to view its progress or find the contact details of your Claims Manager. Here for

body_preview: Good afternoon Rafaella, I wanted to take the time to thank you very much for meeting with Chris, David and myself at your place this afternoon. It was much appreciated. I felt that the meeting was very productive and positive as we all discussed the next steps and have agreed on the claim's progression process moving forward. I would like to re-confirm the next steps we agreed to in writing, and share with all as below: 1. Asset Restore to attend site as soon as possible and complete the process of assessing and cataloging the contents for the response of the contents claim. After assessment the contents which are categorised as a total loss will be removed from site. 2. Asset Restore to strip out the property of any fire or smoke damaged linings or other non-structural items. This will enable a clear and clean structural assessment to be undertaken, from which we will produce a scope of works for the repairs required. 3. Once we have a scope of works completed - we will share this with you to approve before we submit to our repair panel for tendering. Confirming that you have notified us of the access code to be 1106. We will keep you updated on the above process and communicate with you as we move through the above steps. I have spoken with both our Motor and our Claims Team Leaders, and they have said that the items you have enquired about are very unlikely to be affected by the fire given their location with respect to the proximity of the fire. body_preview: Hi Grant, Thank you for your recent email and for the summary of our meeting. I appreciate your efforts to move the claim forward. There are a few important matters I’d like to clarify to ensure the process remains safe, fair, and aligned with Tower’s responsibilities under the policy. First, regarding the items I took with me from the property — including my electric vehicle (EV), laptops, tablets, and mobile phones — these were all exposed to heat, smoke, and corrosive chemicals (including nearby spa products that intensified the fire). I remain concerned about internal damage, particularly to lithium batteries, and the potential for delayed failure or fire. It is not appropriate or reasonable for Tower to shift the responsibility for assessing or managing the risk of these items to me. Ensuring they are safe for continued use is a critical safety issue — not just a claims matter. Tower must take responsibility for confirming whether these items are safe, and whether they require restoration or replacement. Second, I would appreciate clarification of Asset Restore’s scope. Is their role limited to cataloguing and removing contents, or will they also be undertaking removal of fire- or smoke-damaged linings (such as jib and other non-structural internal components)? This detail is important for understanding when and how a proper structural assessment of the property will take place. body_preview: Hello Rafaella, Responding to your email below. I understand that David has responded to acknowledge that there are more contents to schedule and appraise and he is continuing to do so. You have sked me for Towers approval for you to appoint a Structural Engineer to assess the property. There is no requirement for this to be done. As explained in my previous email – we have attended with a technical building Consultant who is well qualified to make the necessary observations and issue documents and instructions on the repairs that are required to complete repairs to your home. I have copied the capability statement directly below which I shared with you last night. Noting that ASURNZ will refer to a CPEng qualified structural engineer only if required after having completed their report. We will of course share the report we receive from ASURNZ with you. To that end Tower will not engage a CPEng qualified structural engineer, until instructed to do so by ASURNZ. Tower will not be obliged to reimburse you if you chose to do this for yourself. I have included an extract below from your policy wording which explains the policy response position on this issue. Of most relevance here is the prefacing wording which states that: ‘ We will pay where necessary ‘. Please refer to policy wording extract below. I have also attached a copy of the full policy wording for your further reference. Further, considering the knowledge and experience provided by ASURNZ personnel regarding the ass

body_preview: Good morning, Raffaella. I hope you are well this morning. You will receive the $9,000 temporary accommodation payment in 3 to 5 working days. The remaining amount under the temporary accommodation benefit is $13,820.62 Is there a time I can contact you this week by phone to finalize your contents claim? the loss schedule provided by Asset Restore to date exceeds your sum insured, which means that the contents is a total loss as the total value of the contents damaged or destroyed is greater then the sum insured. I work until 1:30 so let me know when this week suits you. I have confirmation that your privacy request and the information you requested was emailed to you on the 25/07/25. If you can check this date and let me know if you have it. Regarding updates, I am more then happy to update you on the first of each month, would you prefer monthly contacts from me instead of fortnightly? Regarding further claim files, we can send you assessor reports as we receive these from the assessor. It would be less prudent for us to resend you the entire claim file each month and not efficient for us due to the time it takes to action these we may end up getting assessor reports during this process which means that report may not be added to that privacy request. Future assessor reports should be the only documents we have that you are not given by the assessor as this would come from us. Kind regards, Zach Complex Claims Management Consultant | Tower DDI +64 9 960 4527| 0800 808 808 e

body_preview: Hi Grant, I am following up on our visit to the property on Tuesday. I left the meeting feeling quite confused about several points, and I would be grateful if you could please clarify the following so I can properly understand the approach Tower is taking: 1. Independent expert testing You indicated that Tower/you will not pay for any expert testing as part of my preparation of the claim, as you said that is what you are already doing. However, I am struggling to understand this position. From my perspective, you are preparing the claim from Tower’s side, and I have not been invited to or involved in any of the NZRSC assessments or discussions. This does not feel independent or transparent from my side. Given the seriousness of the fire, and the chemical involvement (including spa chemicals with corrosive chlorides and bromides), I am concerned that critical risks may be under-assessed. Could you please clarify why Tower would not support me in engaging independent engineers, hygienists, or other specialists to provide an impartial view? Further, I request that I be included in all communications with any assessors and 3rd parties— including texts, emails, calls, and meetings — and that these be scheduled so I have the opportunity to participate. This is essential for transparency and to ensure I am properly informed about the basis of decisions being made. 2. body_preview: Hello Rafaella, Please see below [in blue] my responses to the questions in your email below dated September 6, 2025. Please also find attached a copy of the ‘Material Damage Report’ & the ‘Scope of Works’ which relates to the damage report. I have done my best to answer the specific questions that you have raised. My general response to your enquiries is summarised as follows: 1. All material and documentation of any form, in respect of your claim, is discoverable and is available to be provided to you. This will remain your enduring right through-out the claim process. 2. I will need to work closely with your Claim Manager to ensure the information that you are requesting is provided to you in a timely manner. I am wiling to assist the Claims Manager in this respect. 3. All aspects of potential damage which you are seeking to receive assurances on can be responded to on an individual basis. I am unable to provide those responses to you in this email, as I will require consultation and input from the relevant specialists and consultants. I think the most constructive next step would be for me to schedule a meeting with the key stakeholders within Tower Insurance and following that provide you with the clarification that you have requested. Please allow me to schedule a meeting with my colleagues and take the time required to provide you with a considered and comprehensive response. Best Regards, Grant. [cid:image001.png@01DC224D.327AFF60] Grant Metson Regional Manager - Hous

body_preview: Kia ora, Tower team, I’m hoping you can help me straighten something out. Back on 1 July 2025 I asked for a full copy of my claim files for C90306048 and C90306256. Since then, I’ve received a couple of broken ShareFile links and, only just now, a working link with a small bundle of six documents. I may be misunderstanding, but I expected a broader set of records. A few things have left me unsure: Mixed folders – Once I finally received a working link, it showed both a folder with my name and another customer’s name. I’m not sure whether that was intentional or a display error, but it made me nervous that I might be seeing things I shouldn’t—or that others could see mine (see attached screenshot). It seems to be a security and privacy issue. Could you please confirm (a) what occurred, (b) what records were exposed, (c) the containment steps taken. Also I believe you might have make a notifiable privacy breach report to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner and notify the impacted individuals as required Scope/quantity – The six documents look like only a tiny part of the total timeline and scope. I was expecting, to cover the full period not just the very beginning and I was expecting the full claim diary/notes, internal emails, instructions to contractors, quotes/scopes/variations, site photos/videos, test results (e.g., moisture/soot/asbestos), call recordings/transcripts, texts, messages, any engineering or builder assessments, and third‑party correspondence Redactions –

I haven’t obtained professional advice yet, so I reserve the right to ask further questions, seek clarification, and provide additional feedback. Fairness & participation From the ASURNZ report there were two assessment sessions (11 and 21 August). I wasn’t invited to the briefings or either site inspection. I appreciate this may have been an oversight, but it leaves me without visibility of the brief given to ASURNZ and what they actually saw on the day. To balance the process, I kindly propose that Tower funds an independent insured-side assessment with me directly engaged; and that I am permitted a one-on-one session with ASURNZ, as Grant had, so I can brief them directly and ask questions. I’d also be grateful for the raw site notes and photo log from those two attendances, and for inclusion (with reasonable notice) in all future briefings, site visits, and communications. body_preview: Kia ora Raffaella, Thank you for your email, and please accept our sincere apologies for this incident. We really appreciate you bringing this to our attention so promptly. We take customer privacy extremely seriously, and we’ve acted quickly to contain the matter. We can confirm that no one other than your email address accessed your folder. Based on our current assessment, it appears that only you could have entered the file in error, and no information has been exposed. To ensure security, we’ve deleted both folders and created new ones in ShareFile with secure links. We’ve also notified the Office of the Privacy Commissioner and initiated an investigation to understand the root cause. Additionally, we’ve asked the team to review your request thoroughly to ensure nothing has been missed and to provide clarity around the redaction of information. We will get back to you within 5 workdays. Please use the new link below to access your folder. You’ll need to enter your email address, first name, and surname. You can leave the “Company” field blank. https://towernz.sharefile.com/d-s9e40baa94ab04100a6fbeeb731e237e7 Please let me know if you have any problems accessing your folder. body_preview: Kia ora Privacy Team, Thanks for checking in. I can access the link; however, it is not identity-restricted. As a test, I could open the folder using a different, unknown email address simply by entering a name and passing reCAPTCHA. I’ve saved screenshots. Please treat this as a security issue. For clarity, on 1 July 2025 I requested a full copy of my file for both claims (C90306048 and C90306256), “including all internal notes, reports, third-party correspondence, scopes, assessments, emails, and any other material Tower holds in relation to these claims.” Legal effect: Under IPP6 Privacy Act 2020, I’m entitled to access my personal information. “Including” is non-exhaustive, covering all formats and systems (e.g., email, IM/Teams/Slack, call recordings/transcripts, SMS, photos/video, test results, and native costing spreadsheets). It also extends to information held for Tower by its agents/contractors. If anything is withheld or redacted, please identify the specific ground(s). Fair Insurance Code: As a signatory, Tower commits to handling claims and complaints honestly, fairly, transparently, and in a timely way, with clear communication about what you will do and when. The insecure link, incomplete response, and broad redactions to date don’t feel consistent with those commitments. I also want to note the human impact. I’m already dealing with the aftermath of the fire; the insecure link, incomplete response, and redactions have added avoidable stress and time burden. RE

body_preview: Kia ora Grant, thanks for your email and for proposing to bring stakeholders together. As you’ve said, you “ will gather the supporting information… and provide that… well ahead of [any] site meeting,” and you’ll send an agenda based on my email. Could you please first send your written responses to my questions and the supporting documents (reports, scope, raw notes/photo logs from prior attendances, and the test-led plan with methods, thresholds, labs and pass/fail criteria)? Once I have those, I can identify the appropriate experts to attend. Given I rely on third-party availability, please plan on 1–2 weeks’ notice after I receive your written responses and the agenda. I’m very happy to meet then. To avoid further email back-and-forth, please also include: AC2213/Council path (consent or written exemption) and confirmation of CPEng engagement; Hold-points and clearances (roof/eaves intrusives, cavities, corrosion/pH-chloride, IAQ, whole-installation electrical to AS/NZS 3000, HVAC/gas hygiene); Any directions provided to ASURNZ. Once received I’ll revert with attendee names and propose meeting dates within the 1–2 week window. body_preview: Kia ora team, Thank you for your email. I’d like to clarify a few points so we can bring this to a timely conclusion: Timeframes My Privacy Act request was made on 1 July 2025. The statutory timeframe has passed. On 15 September 2025 I was told the additional material would be provided within 5 working days. Could you please explain why the date has now moved to 8 October, and on what basis? Privacy & security There have been several issues to date (broken ShareFile links, visibility of another customer’s folder, and access not tied to my identity). I appreciate that you have notified OPC and recreated folders; however, these incidents, combined with delay, have caused avoidable stress. Please confirm the containment steps taken and that all records relevant to both claims are being preserved. Redactions The material provided so far is heavily redacted without identification of the grounds relied upon. body_preview: Hello Raffaella and Abigail, Thank you for the letter received yesterday at 4:35 I am replying to acknowledge receipt of the letter, and to further acknowledge that Abigail will be copied into all correspondence moving forward. I have requested that the appointed specialists provide dates and times when they can be available. I am proposing that the following persons complete site visit, assessing & testing, as a necessary pre-requisite to providing relevant reports for all stakeholders to review: Grant Metson – Regional Assessing Manager Stephen Walker – Technical Building Consultant. ASURNZ. Warwick Banks – Associate Structural Engineer. CPEng Alexandra Dalzell – Forensic Scientist Tyrell Craig – Restoration Cleaning Specialist. LBP Builder [ unconfirmed individual ] – we will require the builder to cut inspection holes in the wall linings so testing can be completed within the wall cavities. The intention is to test for smoke and chemical contamination within the wall cavities, and on the exposed surfaces downstairs. There will be a significant number of sample sites [tests] to be completed to be able to author a reliable report. After initial testing is completed, our intention is to apply restorative cleaning methods to targeted individual sample sites, followed by subsequent testing, post-cleaning, to provide data to measure the effectiveness of cleaning/decontamination. All samples taken from site will require laboratory analysis before being incorporated into a report

body_preview: Kia ora Grant, Thank you for your email and for confirming receipt of our letter. Friday, 17 October after 2:00 pm works for me, and I’d appreciate it if testing and assessment could be scheduled accordingly. I also reserve my right to undertake independent testing and assessment of the property to ensure a full and impartial evaluation. Having reviewed the proposed plan, I’m concerned the current testing schedule appears incomplete. It does not include the first floor, roof cavity, or the large upper deck, and the basement coverage seems partial, given the known extent of smoke, water, and chemical contamination. I therefore request confirmation that the planned inspection and sampling will be expanded to include all levels and relevant structural areas. Alternatively, please confirm that Tower will meet my reasonable costs should I need to arrange independent specialists to carry out supplementary testing (including laboratory analysis). Please confirm the final timing for the 17th and the revised scope of testing once available. body_preview: Hello Raffaella, Thank you for the email below. We can confirm that testing can commence at 2pm on Friday October 17. We will test from sample sites as required to complete a full and thorough determination of any potential contamination, inclusive of all the areas which you have referred to below. You may undertake your own independent testing at any stage; however, Tower would only consider reimbursement for testing which is - a) claim related & b) testing which is outside of that which Tower will complete. I am confident that the Tower Insurance testing will be transparent, quantified by lab results, and adequate to provide comfort for all parties. Clarified scope of testing as follows: * 150mm diameter inspection portals cut in wall cavities of every room on the first floor at a height of 300 mm of floor level and directly underneath the top plate. * Testing of the roof cavity at the soffit junction where potential contaminants may have risen through the wall cavity. Testing of the roof cavity at the apex where smoke may have accumulated. * Testing of the underside of the rear large upper deck. * Testing of the basement utilizing all exposed framing cavities and 150mm testing portals cut into any remining ceiling linings in the basemen area. * Any additional sample sites that we feel are required during testing. To ensure effectual testing it would be beneficial if you could confirm the following: * That the chlorine was located in the laundry room downstairs at the time

Please confirm if this (and future temporary accommodation payments) can be made into the same account as the previous ones. Once I receive confirmation I will arrange payment urgently, with this usually being processed within 3 – 5 business days. I have also noted that you provided costs for vet fees. These would not be able to be reimbursed as the vet fees are not covered by the policy if your pet is injured in an accident with a motor vehicle. I can see Zach has already advised you that the water rates would not be able to be reimbursed. I also am happy to confirm that future payments for temporary accommodation will be acted upon far more promptly, I recommend sending these to costs to the claims email address, and copying me into it as well. body_preview: Hi Lance / Tower Claims, I’m writing about my temporary accommodation refund submitted on 9 October 2025 under the content insurance claim, which remains unpaid. On 2 September 2025, Tower confirmed the remaining amount under my temporary accommodation benefit was $13,820.62. As at 6 November 2025 (20 business days since my submission), I have received no acknowledgement, no payment, and no progress update, despite repeated follow-ups. While previous refunds of the same were acknowledged within about 3 business days and paid promptly. In line with my policy commitment to prompt payment and the Fair Insurance Code obligations for timely communication, please: Process immediate payment of the temporary accommodation expenses submitted on 9 October 2025; and Confirm the payment date Please also confirm in writing that, going forward, Tower will acknowledge each accommodation refund request within 10 business days, and pay promptly. Kind regards, Raffaella del Prete

Planning ahead for whatever nature throws. ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ If you are unable to see the message below, click here to view . CUSTOMER NUMBER | K40550635422 CUSTOMER NUMBER | K40550635422 Hi Maria, There’s no place quite like yours. It has your style, your stories and your people living in it. That’s what makes it a home. It’s also unique when it comes to natural hazards, like earthquakes, landslides or floods. Some may be more likely, others not so much. So we use risk ratings to help you understand the potential risks we see at your place. 1 Watch the video Watch the video How do risk ratings work? We use data from different sources to calculate the potential risk of four types of natural hazards at your place. Earthquake How likely and damaging might an earthquake be? Landslide Do you live near a cliff or a hill that could cause problems? Flood Might your place flood from heavy rain, and how badly? Sea surge Could unusual tides cause trouble? Your home is given a risk rating for each natural hazard based on how likely we think they are to happen, and how much damage may be caused. 1 What do risk ratings mean for you? We use this data to calculate your specific risk premium, so you pay for natural hazard risks at your place, and not anyone else's. 1 It's a fairer way to charg

It contains the raw data from the laboratory, the results, and the site map and photographs of where the individual samples were taken. As previously discussed, this report is a portion of our response in isolation. It will be followed shortly by: 1. A report from the structural engineer 2. A summary of the proposed strategy / method for decontamination of identified elements of the structure 3. A proposed scope of works for full remediation of the property All the above will be presented to you in a full report which presents the above information in unison and with full context. My intention is to have all the above to you by the end of this week. As always – please do not hesitate to ask if you require further information. Many Thanks, Grant. body_preview: Kia ora Privacy Team, I’m writing regarding my Privacy Act request, originally submitted on 1 July 2025, which remains incomplete and non-compliant after several months, multiple partial disclosures, and repeated extensions. Under IPP6 of the Privacy Act 2020, I am entitled to access all personal information Tower holds about me across all systems and formats, including information held by Tower’s contractors or agents. A brief chronology for clarity: 1 July: Full-file Privacy Act request submitted. 25 July: First ShareFile link received but was broken. 12 September: Public link exposed my folder and another customer’s folder; only six heavily redacted documents were provided. 25 September: Second disclosure provided but still incomplete and missing core categories. 26 Sept → 13 Oct → 24 Oct: Timeframes moved several times without a lawful extension notice. 20 October: Tower requested extension to 7 November, which I accepted in good faith. 7 November: A bundle was provided, but mostly reating exchanges with myself, duplicates and missing substantial categories of information. Since 1 July, Tower has provided broken links, a public ShareFile link that exposed another customer’s folder, incomplete document sets, and multiple shifting deadlines. body_preview: Good afternoon Grant, Thanks for your email below. My client does not accept that this scope includes all the necessary work to reinstate the fire damage at her house. However, in order to keep this claim moving towards resolution, please proceed to cost Tower’s scope of works as you suggest. My client’s experts will review the scope and repair methodology and we will revert. Due to the time of year, this will not be until late January. Some issues which we require expert input on include: 1. Checking the structural methodology 2. Building Consent Costs, including engineering 3. Any consideration of Heritage/Natural Character Overlay requirements specific to 23 Mays Street 4. Any assessment/provisional cost for water pipe damage (informed by the fire service reporting likely damage) 5. Any assessment/provisional cost for gas lines and gas unit 6. Soot, PAH, VOC or other combustion testing for carbon 7. Potential for damage / contamination in wiring 8. Potential knock-on consequences for electrical in the rest of the house 9. Insulation removal/replacement in cavities 10. Moisture mapping 11. Mould assessment or treatment 12. Potential corrosion from chlorides We will let you know when this is completed. Lance, we are waiting for your assessment of temporary accommodation costs. We discussed whether an additional sum could be paid on the basis that there was delay in commissioning testing. Raffaella is now out of pocket for temporary accommodation so your response on this poin

body_preview: Hi, I am writing to follow up on my previous requests, as I have not yet received an acknowledgement or response regarding the refund of my accommodation expenses. Under the policy coverage of NZD 30,000, there is still a remaining balance of NZD 1,820.62. However, reimbursement has only been provided up to 20 November, leaving the remaining amount outstanding. Additionally, on 9 October I submitted a request for reimbursement of NZD 395.85 for emergency veterinary expenses incurred on the day of the fire. body_preview: Good Afternoon Raffaella, I have requested payment is made to you today for the Temporary Accommodation. For the Vet Fees, these are not covered by your policy as your cat was not injured as a result of an accident with a motor vehicle as required by the policy. Veterinary fees for your domestic pet We’ll pay for veterinary fees if your domestic pet is injured in an accident with a motor vehicle. Any claim under this benefit will be excess-free. We pay this benefit in addition to your contents sum insured. Limit We’ll pay up to $500 for each event I have discussed the storage of contents items with our assessing team and have had confirmation that these currently able to be stored safely and securely in the property which we understand is also alarmed. For this reason, we would not currently cover storage costs for you, as there is no need for items to be stored in a commercial storage facility. However, this is likely to change when the repairs actually start, at this time we can review any change in circumstances and reimburse the reasonable costs for items to be stored in a commercial storage facility. Please note that any reimbursement of costs will still need to go through our normal approval process. Hopefully this covers off your questions, however I am more than happy to provide additional clarity if required. body_preview: Hi Lance, Thank you for your response and for arranging the payment of the remaining temporary accommodation amount. I would however like to clarify a few points regarding the storage of contents, the condition of the property, and the veterinary expenses that were submitted previously. Firstly, the property is not alarmed, it does not currently have electricity connected, as it was disconnected following the fire by Fire and Emergency during the incident. For this reason, I was somewhat surprised to read that it had been understood to be alarmed, and I would appreciate clarification as to how that conclusion was reached. Secondly, the property remains smoke-contaminated, including areas upstairs, even several months after the fire. As I am now responsible for arranging and covering the decontamination or disposal of the salvaged items, asking me to move those items back into a property that is not habitable, without electricity, not alarmed, and still affected by smoke and chemical contamination appears unreasonable and inconsistent with fair and reasonable claims handling, particularly as it exposes the items to further risks, contamination and damage. In addition, storing contents in a fire-affected structure that has not been decontaminated does not appear consistent with the purpose of the policy benefit covering temporary storage after an insured loss, which exists to protect contents while the house is being made habitable. We want to let you know about an update to our policy terms for total loss claims. If your vehicle is written off and you make a total loss claim, we no longer refund any remaining premium on your policy. The update was made in October 2024 and keeps us in line with most other Kiwi insurers. We realised this material change wasn't specifically communicated to you when your policy/policies renewed. We’re sorry about this and want to explain what it means for you. Policies affected by this change Personal Motor Policy Number : P00004485168 Personal Motor Policy Number : P00005861782 How it works If your total loss claim is accepted, we’ll pay you a settlement amount, minus any applicable excess. If you've paid your premium in advance, this won’t be refunded. If you pay your premium by instalments, any outstanding payments for the year will be deducted from your settlement amount. This also applies to third-party only policies, if your claim for a total loss under “Accidents caused by an uninsured third party” is accepted. body_preview: Good afternoon Lance, I’ve just tried to call – could you please call when convenient tomorrow. Unfortunately, your letter doesn’t address the specific concerns that Prendos has raised, or provide any consideration of the specific provisions which Tower says exempt all the work. Apart from perhaps the floor joists mentioned at paragraph 4.a. of your letter, which are arguable / discretionary based on damage and interpretation, the rest of the works are specifically excluded from the exemptions in Schedule 1. The general ‘maintenance’ provisions of schedule 1 item 1 do not override the specific exclusions in the rest of the Schedule. Especially when all the work is being undertaken at once. Just one example is the internal load bearing and bracing walls: [cid:image001.jpg@01DCC12C.B12CB050] I will happily talk you through the specific provisions of the Building Act schedule 1 if that would assist, but we do not accept that this work can be carried out without consent. If you have taken expert advice on this consenting, please provide it. Otherwise, please reconsider your position.
